title: Warnings to the Rich and Final Exhortations
subtitle: Judgment on oppressors, patience until the Lord comes, prayer and restoration

heb.paragraph: Come now, you rich, weep and howl for the miseries coming upon you.
Your riches have rotted and your garments are moth-eaten.
The wages of the laborers which you kept back by fraud cry out.

refs.note: You trample on the poor and take from him exactions of wheat.
You shall not oppress a hired worker.
Woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation.

notes.note: Weep and howl for coming miseries. Riches rot, garments moth-eaten. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

You have lived in luxury. You have condemned and murdered the righteous. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin notes that James speaks prophetically. The condemnation is absolute. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: You have stored up treasure in the last days is ironic. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo sees this as prophetic denunciation in the tradition of Amos. Moo's exegesis situates James within the broader wisdom tradition, noting how the author draws on Proverbs, Sirach, and Jesus's own teaching to construct a practical theology of faithful living. The epistle's imperatives are grounded not in legalism but in the transformative reality of the 'implanted word' (1:21).

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney emphasizes the covenantal dimension. Economic injustice is covenant violation. McCartney connects this passage to the OT prophetic tradition, reading James as a continuation of Israel's wisdom and prophetic heritage adapted for the messianic community. The epistle's social ethic is not novel but draws deeply on the Torah's vision of justice, generosity, and covenant solidarity.

hist.historical: James writes to 'the twelve tribes in the Dispersion' — Jewish Christians scattered throughout the Roman Empire after persecution in Jerusalem (Acts 8:1; 11:19). The letter reflects the earliest stage of Christianity when believers still gathered in synagogues (2:2, Greek synagōgē). James, the brother of Jesus, led the Jerusalem church (Acts 15; Gal 2:9) until his martyrdom in AD 62. His letter addresses practical concerns: economic disparity between rich and poor, the relationship between faith and works, and the power of the tongue. The wisdom tradition of Judaism pervades the letter, echoing Proverbs and Jesus' Sermon on the Mount. The agricultural imagery (1:11; 3:12; 5:7) and references to early and late rains reflect the Palestinian context.
hist.context: James turns to rich landowners who oppress workers. This is the harshest passage in the letter. The rich have hoarded wealth while defrauding laborers.

heb.paragraph: Be patient, brothers, until the coming of the Lord.
The coming of the Lord is at hand. The Judge is standing at the door.
You have heard of the steadfastness of Job.

refs.note: The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.
Let your yes be yes and your no be no.

hist.context: James turns from the oppressors to the oppressed. Be patient until the Lord comes. The farmer illustrates patience. Job is an example of steadfastness.

notes.note: MacArthur emphasizes that patience (makrothymia) is not passive resignation but active endurance grounded in eschatological hope. The farmer analogy illustrates the certainty of harvest — believers can wait because they know the Lord's coming is sure. The 'early and late rains' refer to October-November and March-April rains essential for Palestinian agriculture.

The prohibition against grumbling addresses a specific temptation for the oppressed: turning bitterness toward fellow believers. MacArthur notes that the Judge standing at the door means accountability is imminent — for oppressors and grumblers alike.

The prophets exemplify patience under suffering; Job exemplifies steadfastness through suffering. MacArthur distinguishes these: the prophets endured persecution for speaking God's word; Job endured inexplicable loss while maintaining integrity. Both point to God's ultimate compassion and mercy.

The oath prohibition echoes Jesus' teaching (Matt 5:33-37). MacArthur argues James targets manipulative oath-taking that uses divine names to bolster credibility. Simple truthfulness — 'yes' meaning yes — renders oaths unnecessary.

Calvin reads the farmer analogy as a rebuke to impatience. Just as the farmer cannot hasten the seasons, believers cannot hasten the Lord's return. But the farmer's patience is not empty hope — he knows the rains will come. So believers wait with confident expectation, not anxious uncertainty.

Calvin connects the grumbling prohibition to the previous section: those who suffer injustice are tempted to resent those who seem to escape it. But such resentment invites judgment. The prophets and Job teach that God's purposes unfold on His timetable, not ours.

Calvin sees oath-taking as symptomatic of a deeper problem: distrust of simple speech. When words are cheap, oaths seem necessary. But the godly should speak with such integrity that their bare word carries weight. 'Above all' signals the gravity of this command.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Greek makrothymēsate (be patient) is an aorist imperative suggesting decisive commitment to patience as a posture. The 'early and late rains' (proimon kai opsimon) are technical agricultural terms for the autumn and spring rains that bracketed the Palestinian growing season.

The verb stenazō (grumble) carries connotations of sighing, groaning, or complaining — inner resentment that may or may not be voiced. The present imperative with mē prohibits ongoing action: 'stop grumbling' or 'do not keep grumbling.'

The noun hypomonē (steadfastness) differs from makrothymia (patience): the former emphasizes endurance under pressure, the latter emphasizes restraint from retaliation. Job exemplifies hypomonē — he endured without cursing God despite every provocation.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo argues that James's eschatology is thoroughly imminent: the Lord's coming is 'near' (ēngiken), the Judge is 'standing at the door.' This urgency shapes the ethical exhortation — patience is required precisely because the wait is short, not despite it. The nearness of vindication makes present suffering bearable.

Moo notes that James's appeal to the prophets is unusual — most Jewish tradition emphasized their message, not their suffering. But James sees the prophets as moral exemplars whose patience under persecution models the stance believers should adopt. Job's 'end' (telos) refers to his restoration in Job 42.

Moo connects this verse to James's earlier critique of the tongue (3:1-12). Oath-taking represents speech at its most solemn; if even oaths can be abused, how much more ordinary speech? The solution is not more elaborate oaths but more consistent truthfulness.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney emphasizes the agricultural context: James's audience understood the anxious waiting for rain, the vulnerability of crops to drought. The farmer's patience is not stoic detachment but invested hope — everything depends on the rains coming. So believers wait for the Lord with similar investment.

McCartney reads the grumbling prohibition as community-focused: suffering tempts believers to blame each other, to fracture under pressure. But the Judge who will vindicate them against external oppressors will also judge internal divisions. Unity under trial is both witness and survival strategy.

McCartney notes the rhetorical progression: prophets suffered for speaking truth; Job suffered innocently; believers are tempted to speak falsely (oaths). The contrast implies that truthful speech, even when costly, belongs to the pattern of prophetic and Joban faithfulness.

heb.paragraph: Is anyone among you suffering? Let him pray. Is anyone cheerful? Let him sing praise.
Let them anoint him with oil in the name of the Lord.
Confess your sins to one another and pray for one another.

refs.note: Elijah prayed and it did not rain; he prayed again and the heavens gave rain.
The disciples anointed with oil many who were sick.
Love covers a multitude of sins.

notes.note: Suffering calls for prayer; cheerfulness for praise. Sickness calls for the elders.
The prayer of faith will save the sick. Confession and intercession promote healing.
Elijah was a man like us. He prayed fervently. Prayer moves heaven.
If anyone wanders and someone brings him back, he saves a soul from death.

hist.context: The letter concludes with practical instructions on prayer. Suffering? Pray. Cheerful? Sing. Sick? Call the elders. Confess sins to one another.

notes.note: Calvin sees prayer as the comprehensive response to all circumstances: suffering calls for petition, cheerfulness for praise, sickness for the prayers of the church. The elders represent not a clerical order but the mature believers entrusted with pastoral oversight. Anointing with oil was a common medicinal practice, here sanctified by prayer.

Calvin carefully distinguishes this 'prayer of faith' from presumptuous claims. The faith that heals is God's gift, sovereignly granted in some cases and withheld in others. Confession of sins to one another is not sacramental absolution but mutual vulnerability that enables effectual prayer.

Calvin emphasizes that Elijah was 'a man of like nature' — not a superhero but a flawed prophet who elsewhere fled in fear (1 Kings 19). His powerful prayers flowed not from personal holiness but from alignment with God's declared purposes. Any believer similarly aligned can pray with similar effect.

Calvin reads this conclusion as pastoral urgency: the church's mission includes restoring wanderers. 'Covering a multitude of sins' refers not to earning merit but to preventing the cascade of sin that follows apostasy. The one who turns back a wanderer participates in saving work.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb aleipsantes (anointing) is an aorist participle indicating action accompanying the prayer. The preposition en (in/with) before 'oil' is instrumental, while en (in) before 'the name of the Lord' indicates authority. Oil was widely used medicinally (Luke 10:34); here it is combined with prayer as a faith-act.

The phrase hē euchē tēs pisteōs (the prayer of faith) is grammatically a genitive of quality: prayer characterized by faith. The verb sōsei (will save) and egerei (will raise) are future indicatives expressing confident expectation, not absolute promise — context determines application.

The verb exomologeisthe (confess) is present imperative, indicating ongoing practice. The dative allēlois (to one another) emphasizes mutuality — not hierarchical confession to priests but horizontal confession within the community. Deēsis energoumenē (effectual prayer) uses a present passive participle: prayer that is 'being energized' or 'made effective.'

The verb epistrepsas (turns back) is an aorist participle with future force: whoever turns back a sinner... will save. The verb kalypsei (will cover) echoes Proverbs 10:12 and 1 Peter 4:8. The 'multitude of sins' probably refers to the wanderer's sins, now prevented from multiplying.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo argues that James presents a comprehensive theology of prayer for all circumstances. The shift from individual prayer (suffering/cheerful) to community prayer (sickness) reflects escalating need. The elders function not as miracle-workers but as representatives of the praying community, bringing the sick person into the corporate faith of the church.

Moo carefully navigates the healing promise: the 'prayer of faith' is not a technique but a gift, and the promise operates within God's sovereign purposes. The connection between sickness and sin (v.15b-16) reflects OT categories without requiring that all sickness results from specific sin. Confession enables the community's intercession to address any sin-sickness connection that may exist.

Moo sees Elijah as chosen because he prayed for extraordinary things — drought and rain — and received them. The point is not that believers should pray for weather but that prayer aligned with God's purposes, even when those purposes seem impossible, is effectual. Elijah's humanity ('like us') democratizes this power.

Moo reads the conclusion as James's final pastoral concern: the community must pursue wandering members. 'Saving a soul from death' refers to spiritual death (cf. 1:15), and 'covering sins' means preventing further sin, not atoning for past sin. The letter ends with mission, not comfort.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney emphasizes that James's instructions assume a functioning community: elders who can be called, mutual relationships that enable confession. The sick person takes initiative by calling the elders — this is not clerical visitation but communal response to a member's request. Oil functions as a tangible sign of the community's care and faith.

McCartney argues that the healing promise must be read within James's broader theology of prayer (1:5-8; 4:2-3). Prayer offered 'in faith' is prayer aligned with God's will, not prayer that manipulates God. The confession command assumes that unconfessed sin can hinder prayer; community transparency removes that obstacle.

McCartney connects Elijah to the broader prophetic tradition mentioned in v.10. Elijah's prayers were effective not because he was sinless but because he prayed according to God's revealed purpose (the drought was announced before Elijah prayed, 1 Kings 17:1). Believers who pray according to Scripture pray with similar confidence.

McCartney reads the final verses as a charge to the whole community, not just leaders. Every member bears responsibility for wandering siblings. The abrupt ending — no closing greeting — may be intentional: the letter launches readers into mission rather than concluding with comfortable formulas.

themes.note: Justice dominates the chapter. Mercy appears in God compassion and community restoration.

rows.label: vv. 7-11
rows.text: Patience until the Lord comes; Job as example

lit.note: Chapter 5 addresses economic injustice, patient endurance, and community care through prayer and restoration.

discourse.title: James 5 — Warning to Rich, Patience, Prayer, and Restoration
discourse.thesis: The oppressive rich face judgment. Believers should be patient until the Lord's coming, pray in all circumstances, and restore the wandering.

nodes.label: Woe to rich oppressors (5:1-6)
nodes.text: Come now, you rich, weep and howl. Your riches have rotted, your gold corroded. You have laid up treasure in the last days. The wages you withheld from laborers cry out. You have fattened your hearts for slaughter, condemned and murdered the righteous.

nodes.label: Patience until coming (5:7-11)
nodes.text: Be patient until the Lord's coming. See how the farmer waits for the precious fruit of the earth. Establish your hearts, for the coming is at hand. Do not grumble. Take the prophets as an example of suffering and patience. You have seen the purpose of the Lord — he is compassionate and merciful.

nodes.label: Do not swear (5:12)
nodes.text: Above all, do not swear by heaven or earth or any other oath. Let your 'yes' be yes and your 'no' be no, so that you may not fall under condemnation.

nodes.label: Prayer in all circumstances (5:13-18)
nodes.text: Is anyone suffering? Let him pray. Cheerful? Let him sing. Sick? Let him call the elders to pray over him, anointing with oil. The prayer of faith will save the sick. Confess sins to one another and pray for one another. The prayer of a righteous person is powerful — Elijah prayed and it did not rain for three years.

nodes.label: Restore the wandering (5:19-20)
nodes.text: If anyone wanders from the truth and someone brings him back, let him know that whoever brings back a sinner from wandering will save his soul from death and cover a multitude of sins.

discourse.note: The letter ends without formal closing — abruptly calling the community to mutual care for the wandering.

coaching.tip: 'Come now, you rich, weep and howl for the miseries that are coming upon you' (v. 1). James ends as he began: with economics. Hoarded wealth rots, withheld wages cry out, luxury fattens for slaughter. The prophetic tradition lives in James — Amos would recognize this passage. Wealth without justice condemns.

'Is anyone among you sick? Let him call for the elders of the church, and let them pray over him' (v. 14). Sickness prompts communal response: elders, prayer, anointing, confession. Healing is ecclesial, not individual. 'The prayer of a righteous person has great power' (v. 16). James closes with the community's spiritual resources mobilized.

prayer_prompt: As James calls for patience until the Lord's coming and for the prayer of faith over the sick, consider asking God whether you have given up praying about a situation that He has not yet given up on.
